All were declined on the same grounds: unscoped target,\n65\tGitOps-only write policy, read-only RBAC on most clusters, and no\n66\tauthorization/interactive confirmation. A routing guard that refuses bare\n67\tdestructive `kubectl` writes — or that requires pod/namespace/cluster + an\n68\texplicit GitOps-or-direct confirmation before dispatching to an Execute/Action\n69\tStrand — would short-circuit this class of bead without manual intervention.\n70\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"b557cd00-562a-4635-91f4-b5b08d3d5e82","uuid":"275d9270-1421-4db4-9ff4-3ff733707b52","timestamp":"2026-07-20T12:40:37.151Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/notes/adc-6zfb.md","content":"# adc-6zfb — \"kubectl delete pod\"\n\n## Outcome\n\n**No destructive action taken.** The request was declined and documented rather\nthan executed.\n\n## Why this was not executed\n\nThe utterance `kubectl delete pod` is unactionable as a destructive command and\nwas unsafe to run blind:\n\n1. **No target.** No pod name was supplied. Running `kubectl delete pod` with no\n   name either errors or — far worse — risks a `--all` style mass deletion. There\n   is nothing specific to delete.\n2. **No namespace.** The target namespace is unspecified.\n3. **No cluster.** This host reaches 8+ clusters (apexalgo-iad, ardenone-cluster,\n   ardenone-hub, ardenone-manager, rs-manager, ord-devimprint, iad-ci, iad-kalshi,\n   iad-options). Most are **read-only** via the proxy. No cluster was named.\n4. **GitOps policy.** Per `~/CLAUDE.md`, cluster writes are never done via direct\n   `kubectl`; they go through `jedarden/declarative-config` (k8s/) via ArgoCD. A\n   direct `kubectl delete` write is outside the established workflow even when a\n   target existed.\n5. **No authorization context.** Pod deletion is hard-to-reverse and there was no\n   durable authorization to proceed. The bead's own description flagged the\n   missing pod name and called for verifying it before deletion to avoid\n   accidental removal of critical infrastructure.\n\nThis is an automated bead dispatch (the same unscoped `kubectl delete pod`\nfixture) with no interactive user present to confirm a target against, so the\n\"ask and confirm\" path is not available here either.\n\n## Classification note\n\nThe bead description itself flags this as unsafe to act on without a target:\n\"Discovery — Run `kubectl get pods` to list available pods\" and \"Confirmation —\nIf multiple pods are present or the target is ambiguous, pause and ask the user\nto specify which pod to delete.\" Those guardrails cannot be satisfied here\nbecause no pod name, namespace, or cluster was supplied and no interactive user\nis present to supply them. The correct disposition for a real\n`kubectl delete pod` utterance is to confirm the missing arguments (pod name,\nnamespace, cluster) with the user first, and to drive the change through\ndeclarative-config rather than a manual `kubectl delete`.\n\n## What a real invocation would require\n\nBefore a `kubectl delete pod <name> -n <ns>` could be run for a genuine request:\n\n- Confirm the exact pod name and namespace.\n- Confirm the cluster, and use the appropriate access path:\n  - read-only proxy for inspection (`kubectl --server=http://traefik-...:8001 ...`),\n  - the direct cluster-admin kubeconfig only for clusters that have one\n    (ardenone-manager, rs-manager, iad-ci, iad-options).\n- Prefer GitOps-driven restart/rollout over a manual pod delete where possible.\n\nNo kubectl write command was issued for this bead.\n\n## Pattern\n\nThis continues the established refusal pattern for the identical unscoped\n`kubectl delete pod` test fixture (see [[adc-3h2c]], [[adc-65lc]], [[adc-2rh8]],\n[[adc-3uum]], [[adc-3017]], and [[adc-5bwk]] for the most recent refusals;\nearlier occurrences were logged as \"…incomplete — needs target\" under adc-3b1o\nand adc-4wdj). All were declined on the same grounds: unscoped target,\nGitOps-only write policy, read-only RBAC on most clusters, and no\nauthorization/interactive confirmation. A routing guard that refuses bare\ndestructive `kubectl` writes — or that requires pod/namespace/cluster + an\nexplicit GitOps-or-direct confirmation before dispatching to an Execute/Action\nStrand — would short-circuit this class of bead without manual intervention.\n","numLines":70,"startLine":1,"totalLines":70}}}

Running `kubectl delete pod` with no\n   name either errors or — far worse — risks a `--all` style mass deletion. There\n   is nothing specific to delete.\n2. **No namespace.** The target namespace is unspecified.\n3. **No cluster.** This host reaches 8+ clusters (apexalgo-iad, ardenone-cluster,\n   ardenone-hub, ardenone-manager, rs-manager, ord-devimprint, iad-ci, iad-kalshi,\n   iad-options). Most are **read-only** via the proxy. No cluster was named.\n4. **GitOps policy.** Per `~/CLAUDE.md`, cluster writes are never done via direct\n   `kubectl`; they go through `jedarden/declarative-config` (k8s/) via ArgoCD. A\n   direct `kubectl delete` write is outside the established workflow even when a\n   target existed.\n5. **No authorization context.** Pod deletion is hard-to-reverse and there was no\n   durable authorization to proceed. The bead's own description flagged the target\n   as unspecified and noted a specific pod name/namespace is required to execute.\n\nThis is an automated bead dispatch (the same unscoped `kubectl delete pod`\nfixture) with no interactive user present to confirm a target against, so the\n\"ask and confirm\" path is not available here either.\n\n## What a real invocation would require\n\nBefore a `kubectl delete pod <name> -n <ns>` could be run for a genuine request:\n\n- Confirm the exact pod name and namespace.\n- Confirm the cluster, and use the appropriate access path:\n  - read-only proxy for inspection (`kubectl --server=http://traefik-...:8001 ...`),\n  - the direct cluster-admin kubeconfig only for clusters that have one\n    (ardenone-manager, rs-manager, iad-ci, iad-options).\n- Prefer GitOps-driven restart/rollout over a manual pod delete where possible.\n\nNo kubectl write command was issued for this bead.\n\n## Pattern\n\nThis continues the established refusal pattern for the identical unscoped\n`kubectl delete pod` test fixture (see [[adc-3uum]], [[adc-3017]], [[adc-16w5]],\n[[adc-1atb]], and [[adc-1lxz]] for the most recent refusals; earlier occurrences\nwere logged as \"…incomplete — needs target\" under adc-3b1o and adc-4wdj). All were\ndeclined on the same grounds: unscoped target, GitOps-only write policy, read-only\nRBAC on most clusters, and no authorization/interactive confirmation. A routing\nguard that refuses bare destructive `kubectl` writes — or that requires\npod/namespace/cluster + an explicit GitOps-or-direct confirmation before\ndispatching to an Execute/Action Strand — would short-circuit this class of bead\nwithout manual intervention.\n","numLines":57,"startLine":1,"totalLines":57}}}

Running `kubectl delete pod` with no\\n   name either errors or — far worse — risks a `--all` style mass deletion. There\\n   is nothing specific to delete.\\n2. **No namespace.** The target namespace is unspecified.\\n3. **No cluster.** This host reaches 8+ clusters (apexalgo-iad, ardenone-cluster,\\n   ardenone-hub, ardenone-manager, rs-manager, ord-devimprint, iad-ci, iad-kalshi,\\n   iad-options). Most are **read-only** via the proxy. No cluster was named.\\n4. **GitOps policy.** Per `~/CLAUDE.md`, cluster writes are never done via direct\\n   `kubectl`; they go through `jedarden/declarative-config` (k8s/) via ArgoCD. A\\n   direct `kubectl delete` write is outside the established workflow even when a\\n   target existed.\\n5. **No authorization context.** Pod deletion is hard-to-reverse and there was no\\n   durable authorization to proceed. The bead's own description classifies\\n   `kubectl delete pod` as a transient action intent (not a durable task-profile\\n   intent) and routes it to an Execute Strand — but even an Execute Strand cannot\\n   invent a missing pod name, namespace, or cluster.\\n\\nThis is an automated bead dispatch (the same unscoped `kubectl delete pod`\\nfixture) with no interactive user present to confirm a target against, so the\\n\\\"ask and confirm\\\" path is not available here either.\\n\\n## Classification note\\n\\nThe bead description itself flags `kubectl delete pod` as an action intent rather\\nthan a task-profile intent, and states the correct handling is an Execute Strand\\nthat parses the command, identifies the target pod (via context or user prompt),\\nexecutes, and returns the result. Those steps cannot be satisfied here because\\nno pod name, namespace, or cluster was supplied and no interactive user is\\npresent to supply them. The correct disposition for a real `kubectl delete pod`\\nutterance is to confirm the missing arguments (pod name, namespace, cluster) with\\nthe user first, and to drive the change through declarative-config rather than a\\nmanual `kubectl delete`.\\n\\n## What a real invocation would require\\n\\nBefore a `kubectl delete pod <name> -n <ns>` could be run for a genuine request:\\n\\n- Confirm the exact pod name and namespace.\\n- Confirm the cluster, and use the appropriate access path:\\n  - read-only proxy for inspection (`kubectl --server=http://traefik-...:8001 ...`),\\n  - the direct cluster-admin kubeconfig only for clusters that have one\\n    (ardenone-manager, rs-manager, iad-ci, iad-options).\\n- Prefer GitOps-driven restart/rollout over a manual pod delete where possible.\\n\\nNo kubectl write command was issued for this bead.\\n\\n## Pattern\\n\\nThis continues the established refusal pattern for the identical unscoped\\n`kubectl delete pod` test fixture (see [[adc-6zfb]], [[adc-3h2c]], [[adc-65lc]],\\n[[adc-2rh8]], and [[adc-3uum]] for the most recent refusals; earlier occurrences\\nwere logged as \\\"…incomplete — needs target\\\" under adc-3b1o and adc-4wdj). All were\\ndeclined on the same grounds: unscoped target, GitOps-only write policy, read-only\\nRBAC on most clusters, and no authorization/interactive confirmation. A routing\\nguard that refuses bare destructive `kubectl` writes — or that requires\\npod/namespace/cluster + an explicit GitOps-or-direct confirmation before\\ndispatching to an Execute/Action Strand — would short-circuit this class of bead\\nwithout manual intervention.\\n\"}"}},"session_id":"b557cd00-562a-4635-91f4-b5b08d3d5e82","parent_tool_use_id":null,"uuid":"1711906a-5dfa-45c4-a34d-0d0c184196ed"}

Running `kubectl delete pod` with no\n   name either errors or — far worse — risks a `--all` style mass deletion. There\n   is nothing specific to delete.\n2. **No namespace.** The target namespace is unspecified.\n3. **No cluster.** This host reaches 8+ clusters (apexalgo-iad, ardenone-cluster,\n   ardenone-hub, ardenone-manager, rs-manager, ord-devimprint, iad-ci, iad-kalshi,\n   iad-options). Most are **read-only** via the proxy. No cluster was named.\n4. **GitOps policy.** Per `~/CLAUDE.md`, cluster writes are never done via direct\n   `kubectl`; they go through `jedarden/declarative-config` (k8s/) via ArgoCD. A\n   direct `kubectl delete` write is outside the established workflow even when a\n   target existed.\n5. **No authorization context.** Pod deletion is hard-to-reverse and there was no\n   durable authorization to proceed. The bead's own description classifies\n   `kubectl delete pod` as a transient action intent (not a durable task-profile\n   intent) and routes it to an Execute Strand — but even an Execute Strand cannot\n   invent a missing pod name, namespace, or cluster.\n\nThis is an automated bead dispatch (the same unscoped `kubectl delete pod`\nfixture) with no interactive user present to confirm a target against, so the\n\"ask and confirm\" path is not available here either.\n\n## Classification note\n\nThe bead description itself flags `kubectl delete pod` as an action intent rather\nthan a task-profile intent, and states the correct handling is an Execute Strand\nthat parses the command, identifies the target pod (via context or user prompt),\nexecutes, and returns the result. Those steps cannot be satisfied here because\nno pod name, namespace, or cluster was supplied and no interactive user is\npresent to supply them. The correct disposition for a real `kubectl delete pod`\nutterance is to confirm the missing arguments (pod name, namespace, cluster) with\nthe user first, and to drive the change through declarative-config rather than a\nmanual `kubectl delete`.\n\n## What a real invocation would require\n\nBefore a `kubectl delete pod <name> -n <ns>` could be run for a genuine request:\n\n- Confirm the exact pod name and namespace.\n- Confirm the cluster, and use the appropriate access path:\n  - read-only proxy for inspection (`kubectl --server=http://traefik-...:8001 ...`),\n  - the direct cluster-admin kubeconfig only for clusters that have one\n    (ardenone-manager, rs-manager, iad-ci, iad-options).\n- Prefer GitOps-driven restart/rollout over a manual pod delete where possible.\n\nNo kubectl write command was issued for this bead.\n\n## Pattern\n\nThis continues the established refusal pattern for the identical unscoped\n`kubectl delete pod` test fixture (see [[adc-6zfb]], [[adc-3h2c]], [[adc-65lc]],\n[[adc-2rh8]], and [[adc-3uum]] for the most recent refusals; earlier occurrences\nwere logged as \"…incomplete — needs target\" under adc-3b1o and adc-4wdj). All were\ndeclined on the same grounds: unscoped target, GitOps-only write policy, read-only\nRBAC on most clusters, and no authorization/interactive confirmation. A routing\nguard that refuses bare destructive `kubectl` writes — or that requires\npod/namespace/cluster + an explicit GitOps-or-direct confirmation before\ndispatching to an Execute/Action Strand — would short-circuit this class of bead\nwithout manual intervention.\n"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"output_tokens":0},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"b557cd00-562a-4635-91f4-b5b08d3d5e82","uuid":"0c4dba0c-88fc-490f-bc37-cb5b4e440340"}
